currency.name: CFA franc BEAC (XAF)
currency.note: Shared with 5 other Central African nations. Pegged to the euro via French Treasury. Nominally stable but cash shortages are common in Chad.

crypto.note: No regulatory framework. No crypto infrastructure. Internet penetration ~20%. Digital finance barely exists outside N'Djamena.

article:
🧭 **Overview**
Chad is a landlocked Saharan giant — 5th largest country in Africa (1.28M km²) and consistently among the least developed nations on Earth. It borders Libya, Sudan, Central African Republic, Cameroon, Nigeria, and Niger, making it a geopolitical crossroads and buffer zone for Sahel instability. N'Djamena is the capital and only real city. Oil was discovered in 2003 (Chad-Cameroon pipeline) and briefly promised transformation; revenues were largely captured by the state and military. The country has been under Déby family rule since Idriss Déby seized power in 1990. He died fighting rebels at the front in 2021; his son Mahamat Idriss Déby assumed power, nominally ratified by a contested election in May 2024. Chad hosts over 1.1 million refugees from Sudan, CAR, and Nigeria — one of the highest refugee-to-population ratios in the world. Lake Chad, once Africa's 4th largest lake, has shrunk by 90% since the 1960s — one of the world's most visible climate disasters.

👥 **People & vibe**
Chad's ~18 million people are divided among roughly 200 ethnic groups. The north is predominantly Muslim and Arabic-speaking (Zaghawa, Toubou, Arab groups); the south is predominantly Christian or animist (Sara, Mundang). This north-south divide has fueled conflict since independence in 1960. French is the administrative language; Arabic is the lingua franca of trade and Islam. The culture is conservative, tribal, and deeply religious. N'Djamena is dusty and functional — not cosmopolitan. Chadians are resilient and personally hospitable, but the social environment is shaped by decades of war, poverty, and military governance.

[[MAP]]

🌦️ **Climate & landscape**
Three zones from north to south: Saharan (almost no rain, up to 48°C), Sahelian (semi-arid, 300–600mm/year), and Sudanian savanna (600–1,200mm). N'Djamena is Sahelian — brutal dry season (Oct–May, 38–45°C), short rainy season (June–Sept). The landscape includes Sahara dunes in Borkou, the Tibesti volcanic massif (Emi Koussi, 3,415m — Africa's highest volcano), and the shrinking Lake Chad basin. Harmattan dust storms are severe.

🏠 **Housing & settling in**
Virtually all expats are in N'Djamena, in the Ambassades, Sabangali, and Chagoua neighborhoods. Walled compounds with backup generators and water storage are standard. Rents: basic furnished apartment $900–1,600/month; expat villa $2,500–5,000/month. Daily power cuts, trucked water, and slow internet are the norm. Employer-provided housing is standard for humanitarian and diplomatic staff. Outside N'Djamena, zero expat infrastructure exists.

💼 **Work & economy**
The economy runs on oil (60%+ of export revenue), subsistence agriculture (80% of the workforce), and humanitarian aid. Chad receives billions in international assistance annually. For foreigners, viable paths are: UN agencies (UNHCR, WFP, OCHA — massive operations), major INGOs (MSF, IRC, NRC), oil sector (CNPC), or diplomatic missions. Private sector is negligible. Hardship-adjusted humanitarian salaries: $3,500–8,000/month. Corruption is endemic — Transparency International ranks Chad in the bottom 5 globally.

🛂 **Visa & entry**
Visa required for most nationalities (~$130–150). Permit de séjour required for stays over 3 months. Work permits through sponsoring organization. Security clearance from the Ministry of Interior required to travel outside N'Djamena. Many regions are effectively off-limits: Tibesti, eastern Chad (Darfur border), and parts of the Lake Chad basin. Western governments maintain highest-level travel advisories for most of the country.

🏥 **Healthcare**
Chad's healthcare is among the worst globally — WHO ranks it 187/191. Public hospitals are critically under-resourced. All serious conditions require medical evacuation to Cameroon (Yaoundé), Kenya, or France. Life expectancy: ~54 years. Malaria is hyperendemic. Meningitis belt. Yellow fever, cholera, typhoid, and rabies are endemic. Comprehensive international insurance with air evacuation (minimum $1M coverage) is absolutely mandatory.

🚗 **Transport & mobility**
N'Djamena has paved roads in the center — elsewhere it's sand or laterite. A 4x4 is mandatory everywhere outside the capital. Rainy season (June–Sept) makes many routes impassable. No railway. Hassan Djamous Airport connects to Paris (Air France), Addis Ababa (Ethiopian), and Casablanca (RAM). Domestic flights to Abéché and Moundou via charter. Intercity travel: bush taxis or UN/NGO convoys. Armed escorts required in conflict zones.

🍽️ **Food note (national dish)**
The staple is **Boule** (millet or sorghum ball) eaten with **sauce gombo** (okra and meat stew) or **sauce d'arachide** (peanut sauce). **Daraba** (leafy vegetable stew) is also common. In N'Djamena, Lebanese-owned restaurants and Chinese-run spots are the best expat options. Grilled brochettes are the go-to street food; French baguettes remain a colonial legacy staple.

🔎 **Bottom line**
Chad is not a lifestyle destination — it is one of the world's most demanding hardship postings. HDI rank: 190/193. Security, governance, infrastructure, and healthcare are near the global bottom. Those who go are UN/INGO professionals, oil workers, diplomats, journalists, or researchers. What Chad offers is context: it sits at the intersection of the Sudan refugee crisis (1.1M+ displaced people), the Lake Chad ecological collapse, Boko Haram pressure from the west, and Sahel jihadist spillover from Niger and Mali. The post-Déby transition remains fragile. Not for the unprepared — but critical work happens here.

Expat Score — 2.5 / 10

national_dish.name: Boule de mil / Sauce gombo / Daraba
national_dish.note: Millet balls with okra or peanut stew — the daily staple. N'Djamena has Lebanese and Chinese restaurants for variety.

lgbt_acceptance.note: Homosexuality criminalized — up to 3 years imprisonment. Completely taboo culturally and religiously. Zero visibility, zero protections. Absolute discretion mandatory.

personality.name: Idriss Déby
personality.story: Military officer who seized power in December 1990 and ruled Chad for 30 years. An ethnic Zaghawa from the north, he survived 8 major rebel offensives using his tribal network and French military backing. Secured the Chad-Cameroon oil pipeline and positioned Chad as the West's counter-terrorism anchor in the Sahel. Died April 20, 2021 — the day after winning a 6th presidential term — killed commanding troops against the FACT rebel group at the northern front. His son Mahamat took power immediately, bypassing the constitution. Legacy: 30 years of forced stability, oil revenues without development, and a military dynasty in the making.

cards.title: 💧 Lake Chad — a disappearing inland sea
cards.text: Once 25,000 km², Lake Chad has shrunk to under 2,500 km² — a 90% reduction since the 1960s. Climate change, upstream irrigation, and population growth caused the collapse. Thirty million people across 4 countries (Chad, Niger, Nigeria, Cameroon) depend on the basin for survival. Fish stocks have crashed, pastoralists and farmers compete violently for remaining water, and Boko Haram exploits the resulting poverty and displacement. One of the world's clearest climate disaster case studies — almost completely ignored by global media.

cards.title: 🛢️ The oil curse — textbook edition
cards.text: Oil production launched in 2003 via the Chad-Cameroon pipeline ($4.2B, World Bank-backed). The Bank negotiated an unprecedented 'revenue management law' forcing a percentage of funds into health, education, and infrastructure. By 2006, Déby broke the agreement and diverted funds to the military. The Bank withdrew. Production has declined since its 2004 peak. Chad remains at 190/193 on the HDI. Revenue financed the security state, not development. The result is a case study taught in development economics worldwide.

cards.title: 🏜️ Tibesti — Africa's forbidden volcanic fortress
cards.text: The Tibesti massif in far northern Chad is one of the most remote places on Earth. Home to Emi Koussi (3,415m, Africa's highest volcano) and dramatic calderas, it was explored by early 20th-century French expeditions. Today it's controlled by Toubou armed groups, inaccessible to outsiders, and reportedly used as a gold-mining corridor with smuggling routes to Libya. A geological wonder that almost no one has seen in decades.

cards.title: 🌍 1.1 million refugees — the silent mega-crisis
cards.text: Chad hosts over 1.1 million refugees — mostly Sudanese (fleeing the Darfur genocide and the 2023 civil war), plus Central Africans and Nigerians. It has one of the highest refugee-to-GDP ratios in the world. Eastern Chad (Adré, Iriba, Farchana) has become a sprawling humanitarian corridor. UNHCR, WFP, and dozens of NGOs operate here at massive scale. This crisis receives a fraction of the media coverage of European migration stories despite being far larger in human terms.

cards.title: ⚔️ France's last Sahel fortress
cards.text: Chad has been the cornerstone of French military presence in the Sahel for decades. Operation Barkhane was headquartered in N'Djamena until 2022. French troops have militarily intervened to keep Chadian governments alive at least 4 times. As Mali, Burkina Faso, and Niger expelled French forces, Chad became France's last reliable partner in the region. This Françafrique relationship — military protection in exchange for loyalty and resource access — is both Chad's security lifeline and its political cage.

cards.title: 🦁 Zakouma — Africa's conservation comeback story
cards.text: Zakouma National Park in southeastern Chad is a genuine conservation success. When African Parks took over management in 2010, elephant populations had crashed from 4,000 to ~450 due to poaching by Janjaweed militias. By 2024, elephants have recovered to over 1,000. Lions, buffalo, Kordofan giraffes, and giant eland thrive. It's remote (charter flights from N'Djamena), expensive, and extraordinary — proof that conservation works with proper funding and management, even in the world's most difficult environments.
